html:
[1217]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 庚辰会试 - Imperial examination held in the year of the Fire Dragon (庚辰).
  • 以蓝笔画牡丹 - Literally 'to draw peonies with blue ink,' a specific artistic act.
  • 遍赠同事 - Means 'to give to all colleagues,' indicating a gesture of sharing.
  • 遂递相题咏 - Means 'thus they took turns inscribing and composing verses,' describing a collaborative literary activity.
  • 拨出卷最多 - Means 'selected the most number of scrolls for advancement,' referring to the role of an examiner.
  • 朱石君拨入卷最多 - Similar to the previous phrase, but refers to '朱石君' selecting the most scrolls for elimination.
  • 余题晴沙画曰 - Means 'I inscribed on Con's painting, saying,' indicating the author's contribution.
  • 深浇春水细培沙 - Poetic expression meaning 'to deeply irrigate with spring water and carefully cultivate the sand,' metaphorically describing the nurturing of a flower.
  • 养出人间富贵花 - Means 'to nurture the most splendid flower of human society,' a metaphor for the peony.
  • 好似艳阳三四月 - Means 'like the bright sun in the third or fourth month,' referring to the spring season.
  • 余香风送到邻家 - Means 'the lingering fragrance carried by the wind to the neighboring houses,' a poetic way of describing the peony's scent.
  • 边秋崖前辈和余韵曰 - Means 'Bian Qiu Yan, the senior, responded with a verse,' indicating a collaborative literary exchange.
  • 一番好雨净尘沙 - Means 'a fine rain cleanses the dust and sand,' a poetic description of a rain shower.
  • 春色全归上苑花 - Means 'the entire spring beauty belongs to the flowers in the imperial garden,' a metaphor for the peony.
  • 此是沉香亭畔种 - Means 'these are the flowers planted by the side of the Sinking Fragrance Pavilion,' referring to a famous imperial garden.
  • 莫教移到野人家 - Means 'do not let them be moved to the homes of common people,' a poetic way of emphasizing the peony's noble status.
  • 又题石君画曰 - Means 'I also inscribed on Shi Jun's painting, saying,' indicating another contribution.
  • 乞得仙园花几茎 - Means 'to beg for a few stems of flowers from the heavenly garden,' a poetic way of asking for something precious.
  • 嫣红姹紫不知名 - Means 'vividly red and purple, yet unnamed,' describing the peony's beauty.
  • 何须问是谁家种 - Means 'why ask whose garden they are from,' a poetic way of emphasizing the peony's universal beauty.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 庚辰会试 - Refers to a specific imperial examination, a key event in the Chinese civil service examination system.
  • 钱箨石前辈 - Likely a historical figure, a respected scholar or official.
  • 顾晴沙员外 - Likely a historical figure, an official or scholar.
  • 朱石君 - Likely a historical figure, an examiner or scholar.
  • 边秋崖前辈 - Likely a historical figure, a senior scholar or official.
  • 牡丹 - The peony, a symbol of wealth and nobility in Chinese culture.
  • 沉香亭畔种 - Refers to the Sinking Fragrance Pavilion, a famous imperial garden.
  • 仙园 - A metaphor for a heavenly or noble garden.

Proper Nouns

  • 庚辰 - The year of the Fire Dragon in the Chinese calendar.
  • 钱箨石 - A proper name, likely a historical figure.
  • 顾晴沙 - A proper name, likely a historical figure.
  • 朱石君 - A proper name, likely a historical figure.
  • 边秋崖 - A proper name, likely a historical figure.
  • 牡丹 - The peony, a symbol of wealth and nobility.
  • 沉香亭 - The Sinking Fragrance Pavilion, a famous imperial garden.

[1218]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 石君自和之曰 - Poetic or literary reply indicating a response from Stone Master.
  • 倾国何曾一问名 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'How often has a nation been turned upside down by her, yet no one ever asked her name?' Refers to the beauty of the subject, possibly a woman, whose name is unknown despite her fame.
  • 心似维摩老居士 - Reference to Vimalakirti, a lay Buddhist sage, meaning 'His heart is like that of the elderly layman Vimalakirti,' suggesting a detached, enlightened state.
  • 天花来去不关情 - Refers to the Buddhist story of Vimalakirti, where celestial flowers fall without attaching to anything, symbolizing non-attachment and transcendence.
  • 张镜壑前辈继和曰 - Another poet's response, indicating 'Zhang Jinghe, the senior, followed with his own composition.'
  • 墨捣青泥砚碩沙 - Poetic description of grinding ink in a stone mortar, using '青泥' (blue mud) and '砚碩沙' (inkstone sand) to evoke the process of creating ink.
  • 浓蓝写出洛阳花 - Means 'Deep blue writes out the flowers of Luoyang,' likely referring to the famous peonies of Luoyang, a symbol of beauty and elegance.
  • 云何不著胭脂染 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Why not dye it with rouge?' Suggests the addition of color to enhance beauty.
  • 拟把因缘问画家 - Means 'I would ask the painter about the karmic connections,' implying a discussion of artistic inspiration or the relationship between art and life.
  • 黛为花片翠为茎 - Means 'Black as the flower petals, green as the stem,' describing the colors of the flower.
  • 欧谱知居第几名 - Refers to the 'Ou谱' (a work on flower classification), asking about the ranking or classification of the flower.
  • 却怪玉盘承露冷 - Means 'But I wonder why the jade plate holding the dew is cold,' a metaphorical expression that may refer to a cold, detached state or a symbolic representation of purity.
  • 香山居士太关情 - Refers to Bai Juyi, the 'Fragrant Hill Recluse,' suggesting that he is too emotionally involved, contrasting with the earlier detachment.
  • 多年密友，脱略形骸 - Means 'Long-time close friends, who are free and unconstrained,' describing a relationship where formalities are ignored.
  • 互以虐谑为笑乐 - Means 'They take each other's teasing and jesting as amusement,' indicating a playful, humorous relationship.
  • 初无成见于其闻也 - Means 'They had no preconceived notions about each other,' suggesting a lack of bias or judgment.
  • 蒋文恪公时为总裁 - Means 'Jiang Wenke, at the time, was the president,' referring to Jiang's position of authority.
  • 诸君子跌宕风流 - Means 'The gentlemen are unrestrained and elegant,' describing their free-spirited and refined nature.
  • 古人嫌隙，多起于俳谐 - Means 'Ancient people often developed animosity from playful jokes,' warning against the dangers of humor.
  • 不如并此无之，更全交之道耳 - Means 'It would be better not to have this, to preserve the integrity of friendship,' suggesting that avoiding humor can maintain relationships.
  • 皆深佩其言 - Means 'All deeply admired his words,' indicating agreement with Jiang's advice.
  • 盖老成之所见远矣 - Means 'This shows the wisdom of the experienced,' praising Jiang's insight.
  • 录之以志少年绮语之过 - Means 'Record this to remember the mistakes of youth,' suggesting that the passage serves as a lesson.
  • 后来英俊慎勿效焉 - Means 'Later generations of talented people should not follow this example,' advising against repeating the mistakes.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 维摩老居士 - Refers to Vimalakirti, a lay Buddhist sage who is a central figure in the *Vimalakirti Sutra*.
  • 张镜壑 - A historical figure, likely a poet or scholar, whose works are referenced in the text.
  • 洛阳花 - Refers to the peonies of Luoyang, which are famous for their beauty and are often associated with elegance and refinement.
  • 欧谱 - Likely refers to a work on flower classification, possibly by the Tang Dynasty poet and botanist, Ouyang Xun.
  • 香山居士 - Refers to Bai Juyi, a famous Tang Dynasty poet who lived as a recluse on Fragrant Hill.
  • 蒋文恪公 - Refers to Jiang Wenke, a historical figure who held a high-ranking position, likely during the Qing Dynasty.
  • 绮语 - Refers to 'flowery or vain words,' often associated with youthful indiscretion or frivolous speech.

Proper Nouns

  • 石君 - A poet or scholar, identity not specified.
  • 维摩老居士 - Vimalakirti, a lay Buddhist sage.
  • 张镜壑 - A historical figure, likely a poet or scholar.
  • 洛阳花 - Peonies of Luoyang, symbolizing beauty and elegance.
  • 欧谱 - Likely a work on flower classification.
  • 香山居士 - Bai Juyi, a famous Tang Dynasty poet.
  • 蒋文恪公 - Jiang Wenke, a historical figure who held a high-ranking position.

[1219]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 科场填榜完时 - Refers to the completion of posting the examination results in the imperial examination hall.
  • 必卷而横置于案 - Describes the action of rolling up the list and placing it horizontally on the table.
  • 总裁主考，具朝服九拜 - Indicates that the chief examiner and the chief examiner-in-chief must wear formal court attire and perform nine bows.
  • 然后捧出 - Means to then carry it out.
  • 堂吏谓之拜榜 - Refers to the court officials calling this act 'bowing to the list.'
  • 此误也 - Means 'this is a mistake.'
  • 以公事论 - Means 'from the perspective of official duties.'
  • 一榜皆举子 - Means 'all the names on the list are candidates.'
  • 试官何以拜举子 - Asks 'why would the examiners bow to the candidates?'
  • 以私谊论 - Means 'from the perspective of personal relationships.'
  • 一榜皆门生 - Means 'all the names on the list are my students.'
  • 座主何以拜门生哉 - Asks 'why would the chief examiner bow to his students?'
  • 或证以周礼拜受民数之文殊为附会 - Means 'some try to justify this with the text about the seven days of bowing and receiving the census, which is a stretch.'
  • 盖放榜之日，当即以题名录进呈 - Means 'on the day the list is posted, the list of successful candidates should be presented immediately.'
  • 录不能先写 - Means 'the list cannot be written first.'
  • 必拆卷唱一名，榜填一名，然后付以填榜之纸条，写录一名 - Describes the process of opening the examination papers, announcing one name, filling in the list, and then providing a slip of paper to write down the name.
  • 今纸条犹谓之录条 - Means 'now the slip of paper is still called a 'recording slip.'
  • 必拜而送之，犹拜摺之礼也 - Means 'one must bow and send it, just like the ritual of bowing to a memorial.'
  • 榜不放，录不出 - Means 'the list is not posted, and the recording slips are not issued.'
  • 录不成，榜不放 - Means 'the recording slips are not completed, and the list is not posted.'
  • 故录与榜必并陈于案，始拜 - Means 'therefore, the recording slips and the list must be placed together on the table before the bowing.'
  • 榜大录小 - Means 'the list is large, and the recording slips are small.'
  • 灯光晃耀之下，人见榜而不见录 - Means 'under the bright light, people see the list but not the recording slips.'
  • 故误认为拜榜也 - Means 'so it is mistakenly believed to be bowing to the list.'
  • 厥后或缮录未完，天已将晓 - Means 'later, sometimes the recording is not finished, and dawn is approaching.'
  • 或试官急于复，命先拜而行 - Means 'or the examiners are in a hurry to return, and they order the bowing to proceed first.'
  • 遂有拜时不陈录于案者 - Means 'so there are times when the recording slips are not placed on the table during the bowing.'
  • 久而视为固然 - Means 'over time, this becomes seen as a natural thing.'
  • 堂吏或因可无录而拜，遂竟不陈录 - Means 'the court officials may bow without the recording slips, so they no longer place them on the table.'
  • 又因录既不陈，可暂缓而追送 - Means 'and because the recording slips are not placed, they can be delayed and sent later.'
  • 遂至写榜竣后，无录可陈，而拜遂潜移于榜矣 - Means 'so after the list is written, there are no recording slips to place, and the bowing has quietly shifted to the list.'
  • 尝以问先师阿文勤公 - Means 'I once asked my former teacher, Mr. Aw Wenqin.'
  • 公述李文贞公之言如此 - Means 'he recounted what Mr. Li Wenzheng said.'
  • 文贞即公己丑座主也 - Means 'Mr. Li Wenzheng was the chief examiner of Mr. Aw in the year of Gengchou.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 科场 - Refers to the imperial examination hall where the examinations were held.
  • 榜 - Refers to the list of successful candidates in the imperial examinations.
  • 主考 - Refers to the chief examiner-in-chief.
  • 总裁 - Refers to the chief examiner.
  • 朝服 - Refers to formal court attire.
  • 九拜 - Refers to the ritual of performing nine bows, a sign of deep respect.
  • 堂吏 - Refers to court officials.
  • 周礼拜受民数之文 - Refers to the text about the seven days of bowing and receiving the census, a ritual in ancient China.
  • 题名录 - Refers to the list of successful candidates.
  • 拜摺 - Refers to the ritual of bowing to a memorial, a formal document presented to the emperor.
  • 阿文勤公 - Refers to Mr. Aw Wenqin, a respected scholar and teacher.
  • 李文贞公 - Refers to Mr. Li Wenzheng, a respected scholar and chief examiner.

[1220]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 翰林院堂不启中门，云启则掌院不利 - Superstitious belief that opening the central door of the Hall of the Hanlin Academy would bring misfortune to the president of the academy.
  • 癸已开四库全书馆，质郡王临视 - Refers to the opening of the Siku Quanshu in the year of Gui-Si (癸巳), with Prince Zhi (质郡王) overseeing the event.
  • 司事者启之，俄而掌院刘文正公，觉罗奉公相继逝 - After the door was opened, both President Liu Wenzheng Gong (刘文正公) and Prince Jueluo Fong Gong (觉罗奉公) died shortly thereafter, reinforcing the superstition.
  • 门前沙堤中，有土凝结成丸，傥或误碎必损翰林 - Belief that the soil on the sand embankment in front of the gate forms into balls, and if these balls are accidentally broken, it will harm the Hanlin scholars.
  • 癸未雨水冲激露其一，为儿童掷裂，吴云岩前辈旋殁 - In the year of Gui-Wei (癸未), rainwater washed away one of these balls, and a child broke it, leading to the death of the elder Wu Yunyan (吴云岩).
  • 原心亭之西南隅，翰林有父母者，不可设坐，坐则有刑碠 - Belief that in the southwest corner of Yuanxin Pavilion, Hanlin scholars with parents should not sit, as doing so would bring misfortune.
  • 陆耳山时为学士，毅然不信，竟丁外艰 - Lu Ershan (陆耳山), a scholar-official, refused to believe this superstition and later experienced the death of his father, fulfilling the prediction.
  • 至左角门久闭不启，启则司事者有谴谪，无人敢试，不知果验否也 - The left side gate has been closed for a long time, and if it is opened, the person in charge will face punishment. No one dares to test this, leaving its truth unknown.
  • 其余部院，亦各有禁忌 - Other departments also have their own taboos.
  • 如礼部甬道屏门，旧不加搭渡，搭渡以夹木二方，夹于门限，坡陀如桥状，使堂官乘车者，可从中入，以免于旁绕 - The screen door of the Li Department's corridor used to have no bridge, but now it has a wooden bridge that allows officials to drive their carriages directly through, avoiding the need to go around.
  • 钱箨石前辈不听，旋有天坛灯杆之事者 - Qian Tuoshi (钱箨石), an elder, ignored this practice, and shortly after, there was an incident involving the lamp pole at the Temple of Heaven, reinforcing the belief in the importance of following such practices.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 翰林院 - The Hanlin Academy, a prestigious institution in the imperial court responsible for literary and scholarly tasks.
  • 四库全书馆 - The Siku Quanshu (Complete Library in Four Sections) was a massive compilation of Chinese literature and scholarship during the Qing dynasty.
  • 质郡王 - Prince Zhi, a member of the imperial family who oversaw the opening of the Siku Quanshu.
  • 刘文正公 - President Liu Wenzheng Gong, a prominent figure in the Hanlin Academy.
  • 觉罗奉公 - Prince Jueluo Fong Gong, another high-ranking official.
  • 吴云岩 - An elder scholar, Wu Yunyan, who died after the ball was broken.
  • 陆耳山 - Lu Ershan, a scholar-official who disbelieved in the superstition and later experienced the death of his father.
  • 礼部 - The Ministry of Rites, one of the six ministries in the imperial government, responsible for rituals, ceremonies, and cultural affairs.
  • 天坛灯杆 - The lamp pole at the Temple of Heaven, a significant religious and ceremonial site.

Proper Nouns

  • 翰林院 - The Hanlin Academy.
  • 四库全书馆 - The Siku Quanshu (Complete Library in Four Sections).
  • 质郡王 - Prince Zhi.
  • 刘文正公 - President Liu Wenzheng Gong.
  • 觉罗奉公 - Prince Jueluo Fong Gong.
  • 吴云岩 - Wu Yunyan.
  • 陆耳山 - Lu Ershan.
  • 礼部 - The Ministry of Rites.
  • 天坛灯杆 - The lamp pole at the Temple of Heaven.

[1221]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 相传 - Means 'it is said' or 'according to legend.'
  • 翰林院 - Refers to the Hanlin Academy, an elite scholarly institution.
  • 宝善亭 - A specific location within the Hanlin Academy.
  • 狐女 - Refers to a female fox spirit, often depicted as beautiful and mischievous in Chinese folklore.
  • 二姑娘 - A nickname or title for the fox spirit.
  • 惟 - Means 'only' or 'merely.'
  • 诸筠心学士 - The name of a scholar.
  • 斋宿 - Means 'to stay in a study room.'
  • 梦一丽人携之行 - Means 'dreamed of a beautiful woman taking him along.'
  • 逾越 - Means 'to leap over' or 'to cross.'
  • 墙壁 - Means 'walls.'
  • 如踏云雾 - Means 'as if stepping on clouds and mist,' describing a light and effortless movement.
  • 至 - Means 'to' or 'arrive at.'
  • 城根 - Means 'at the foot of the city wall.'
  • 高丽馆 - Refers to a place associated with Korea, possibly a diplomatic or cultural institution.
  • 遇一老叟 - Means 'met an old man.'
  • 惊曰 - Means 'exclaimed in surprise.'
  • 此褚学士 - Refers to the scholar.
  • 二姑娘何造次乃耳 - Means 'Second Miss, why are you so hasty?'
  • 速送之归 - Means 'quickly send him back.'
  • 遂霍然醒 - Means 'suddenly woke up.'
  • 筠心在清碪堂，曾自言之 - Means 'Once spoke of it himself in the Qingzhen Hall.'
  • 神奸 - Refers to a cunning and deceitful person.
  • 机巧 - Means 'cleverness' or 'trickery.'
  • 有时败也 - Means 'sometimes it fails.'
  • 多财 - Means 'wealthy.'
  • 恣横 - Means 'arrogant' or 'reckless.'
  • 以神奸用其财 - Means 'using wealth with cunning and deceit.'
  • 以多财济其奸 - Means 'using wealth to aid in deceit.'
  • 斯莫可究诘矣 - Means 'thus it cannot be fully investigated.'
  • 景州 - A place name, likely referring to a region in China.
  • 李露园 - A person's name, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
  • 燕齐间 - Refers to the region between the ancient states of Yan and Qi.
  • 里人 - Means 'a villager' or 'a local resident.'
  • 新妇 - Means 'new wife,' often used in the context of a recently married woman.
  • 舅姑 - The husband's parents, specifically the mother-in-law and father-in-law.
  • 阴遣一媪税屋与邻 - Means 'secretly sent an old woman to rent a house next door.'
  • 百计游说 - Means 'all sorts of schemes and persuasion.'
  • 厚赂 - Means 'lavish bribes.'
  • 使以不孝出其妇 - Means 'made them expel her as ungrateful.'
  • 约勿使其子知 - Means 'agreed not to let her son know.'
  • 又别遣一媪与妇家素往来者 - Means 'and sent another old woman who had previously been in contact with the wife's family.'
  • 以厚赂游说其父母 - Means 'with lavish bribes to persuade her parents.'
  • 伪送妇还 - Means 'pretended to send her back.'
  • 舅姑亦伪作悔意 - Means 'the in-laws also pretended to regret it.'
  • 留之饭 - Means 'kept her for a meal.'
  • 已呼妇入室矣 - Means 'already called her into the room.'
  • 俄彼此语相侵 - Means 'soon they started arguing.'
  • 仍互诟 - Means 'still quarreling.'
  • 逐妇归 - Means 'sent her back home.'
  • 亦不使妇知 - Means 'also did not let the wife know.'
  • 于是买休卖休 - Means 'bought and sold the divorce.'
  • 与母家同谋之事 - Means 'the collusion with the mother-in-law's family.'
  • 俱无迹可寻矣 - Means 'all traces were gone.'
  • 既而二媪诈为媒 - Means 'afterward, the two old women pretended to be matchmakers.'
  • 与两家议婚 - Means 'arranged a marriage with the two families.'
  • 富室以惮其不孝辞 - Means 'the wealthy household refused due to fear of being ungrateful.'
  • 妇家又以贫富非偶辞 - Means 'the wife's family refused due to the difference in wealth.'
  • 于是谋取之计亦无迹可寻矣 - Means 'thus the plan to take her was also untraceable.'
  • 迟之又久 - Means 'after a long time.'
  • 复有亲友为作合 - Means 'again, friends and relatives arranged the marriage.'
  • 仍委禽焉 - Means 'they still agreed to the match.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 翰林院 - The Hanlin Academy, an elite scholarly institution in imperial China.
  • 宝善亭 - A specific location within the Hanlin Academy.
  • 狐女 - A female fox spirit, often depicted as beautiful and mischievous in Chinese folklore.
  • 褚学士 - A scholar, likely a reference to a historical figure or a fictional character.
  • 清碪堂 - A specific location, possibly a study room or a hall.
  • 景州 - A place name, likely referring to a region in China.
  • 李露园 - A person's name, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
  • 燕齐间 - The region between the ancient states of Yan and Qi.
  • 高丽馆 - A place associated with Korea, possibly a diplomatic or cultural institution.
  • 里人 - A villager or a local resident.
  • 新妇 - A new wife, often used in the context of a recently married woman.
  • 舅姑 - The husband's parents, specifically the mother-in-law and father-in-law.
  • 买休卖休 - A term used in the context of divorce, where the divorce is bought and sold.
  • 委禽 - A term used in the context of marriage, where the groom sends a gift to the bride's family to formalize the engagement.

[1222]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 其夫虽贫，然故士族 - Contrasts social status with economic condition: 'Though poor, he was of noble lineage.'
  • 无罪弃妇 - Reflects moral judgment: 'To abandon a wife without cause.'
  • 破镜再合 - Metaphor for reunion of separated spouses: 'To reunite broken mirrors.'
  • 合卺之夕 - Refers to the wedding night: 'The night of the wedding.'
  • 同衾枕 - Euphemism for sexual intimacy: 'To share the same bed and pillow.'
  • 改卜其昼 - Indicates a shift from night to day: 'To change the time to daytime.'
  • 新夫如是者 - Implies inappropriate behavior: 'To be with a new husband in such a manner.'
  • 三日新妇 - Refers to the traditional period of mourning and seclusion after marriage: 'A newlywed wife of three days.'
  • 延术士劾治 - Attempt to address the supernatural: 'To invite a magician to exorcise.'
  • 驱邪魅 - Common practice in dealing with supernatural disturbances: 'To drive away evil spirits.'
  • 驱冤魂 - Spirit driven by injustice: 'To drive away a wronged spirit.'
  • 延僧礼忏 - Another attempt to address the supernatural: 'To invite a monk to perform a confession.'
  • 素颇孝 - Consistent trait of being respectful to parents: 'He was always filial.'
  • 出妇不敢阻 - Ease with which the divorce was carried out: 'To divorce a wife without hindrance.'
  • 赂妇之舅姑 - Financial transaction to influence the situation: 'To bribe the husband's in-laws.'
  • 见逐，无复住理 - Finality of the expulsion: 'To be expelled, with no reason to stay.'
  • 讼诸地下 - Metaphor for seeking justice in the spiritual realm: 'To sue in the afterlife.'
  • 僧礼忏亦无验 - Failure of another supernatural intervention: 'The monk's confession also proved ineffective.'
  • 巧安在哉 - Rhetorical question expressing skepticism: 'Where is the cleverness?'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 士族 - Refers to the gentry class, a social stratum in ancient China known for its scholarly and aristocratic status.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 輪迴之說，凿然有之 - The doctrine of reincarnation is clearly and certainly true. '凿然' is a classical expression meaning 'clearly, distinctly,' often used to emphasize certainty.
  • 恒兰台之叔父，生数岁，即自言前身为城西万寿寺僧 - Narrative construction typical of Classical Chinese, where the subject's identity is introduced before the action.
  • 取笔粗画其殿廊门径，庄严陈设，花树行列 - '粗画' means 'roughly sketch' or 'draw in a rough manner.'
  • 往验之，一一相合 - Standard formula in classical narrative for confirming a claim through observation.
  • 朱子所谓轮回虽有，乃是生气未尽，偶然与生气凑合者，亦实有之 - '生气' is a classical term for 'vital energy' or 'life force,' often used in Neo-Confucian philosophy.
  • 余崔庄佃户商龙之子甫死，即生于邻家 - Common classical structure for reporting miraculous or supernatural events.
  • 未弥月能言 - '弥月' refers to the first month of life, so '未弥月' means 'before the first month.'
  • 元旦父母偶出，独此儿在襁褓 - '元旦' is the first day of the year, traditionally New Year’s Day in the lunar calendar.
  • 有同村人叩门，云贺新岁 - '贺新岁' is a traditional greeting for the New Year.
  • 儿识其语音，遽应曰：是某丈耶？ - '遽应' means 'immediately responded.' '某丈' is a respectful term for a man, used here to refer to the villager.
  • 闻者骇笑，然不久夭逝 - '骇笑' is a compound word meaning 'startled laughter' or 'laughter mixed with surprise.'
  • 天下之理无穷，天下之事亦无穷，未可据其所见，执一端论之 - Philosophical conclusion emphasizing the limitations of human knowledge and the need for openness to multiple perspectives.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 轮回 - Reincarnation or transmigration of souls, a concept common in Buddhist and Daoist thought, as well as in Chinese folk beliefs.
  • 朱子 - Respectful reference to Zhu Xi (1130–1200), a leading Neo-Confucian philosopher of the Song Dynasty.
  • 万寿寺 - Wanshou Temple, a real temple located in the western part of Beijing.
  • 崔庄佃户 - Tenant farmer from Cui Zhuang, reflecting the social structure of rural China during the Qing Dynasty.
  • 元旦 - New Year’s Day, the first day of the lunar calendar, a major traditional Chinese holiday.
  • 襁褓 - Swaddling clothes, a term used to refer to an infant, especially in classical texts.
  • 夭逝 - Died young or premature death, a common expression in classical Chinese to describe the death of a child.
  • 未可据其所见，执一端论之 - Philosophical stance common in classical Chinese literature and thought, emphasizing the need for humility in judgment and the recognition of the limits of human knowledge.

Proper Nouns

  • 恒兰台 - Heng Lantai, likely the name of the author’s relative or a person mentioned in the text.
  • 朱子 - Zhu Xi, a well-known Neo-Confucian philosopher.
  • 万寿寺 - Wanshou Temple, a specific temple in Chinese history and geography.
  • 崔庄 - Cui Zhuang, a place name, likely a village or locality near the author’s residence.
  • 商龙 - Shang Long, the name of the tenant farmer.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 門人邱人龍言 - A student named Qiu Renlong said. '門人' specifically refers to a disciple or student in a scholarly context.
  • 舟泊滩河 - The boat was moored at a riverbank. '滩河' (tānhé) refers to a river with sandbars or shallow areas, which could be translated as 'a sandy riverbank' or 'a shallow river.'
  • 夜半有数盗执炬露刃入 - At midnight, several thieves entered with torches and exposed their weapons. '露刃' (lùrèn) is a vivid description of the thieves brandishing their weapons, emphasizing their threat.
  • 众皆慑伏 - Everyone was terrified and submitted. '慑伏' (shèfú) conveys a sense of both fear and submission, indicating that the victims were not only afraid but also compelled to comply.
  • 愿乞夫人一物 - I wish to ask for one thing from Madam. '愿乞' (yuànxī) is a polite and formal way of making a request, which adds a layer of politeness to the thieves' actions.
  • 愿乞夫人一物，夫人勿惊 - I wish to ask for one thing from Madam, Madam, please do not be alarmed. The repetition of '夫人' (fūrén) emphasizes the address to the woman, making it clear that the request is directed at her.
  • 即割一左耳 - They immediately cut off one of her left ears. '割' (gē) is a strong verb meaning 'to cut off' or 'to sever,' which adds to the intensity of the action.
  • 敷以药末 - They applied some medicine powder. '敷以' (fūyǐ) means 'to apply' or 'to spread,' and '药末' (yào mò) refers to powdered medicine, which suggests a specific treatment.
  • 数日勿洗，自结痂愈也 - Do not wash it for several days; it will naturally form a scab and heal. '自结痂愈' (zì jié jiā yù) is a medical term indicating that the wound will heal naturally without further intervention.
  • 遂相率呼啸去 - They then left, shouting and laughing. '呼啸' (hūxiào) conveys a sense of noise and laughter, suggesting that the thieves left in a boisterous manner.
  • 怖几失魂 - She was so terrified that she almost lost her soul. '怖几失魂' (bù jī shī hún) is a hyperbolic expression indicating extreme fear, which adds to the emotional impact of the story.
  • 以为仇耶？不杀不淫 - Was this an act of revenge? They did not kill or rape. '以为仇耶' (yǐ wéi chóu yé) is a rhetorical question, and '不杀不淫' (bù shā bù yín) emphasizes the thieves' restraint.
  • 以为盗耶？未劫一物 - Was this an act of theft? They did not take anything. '未劫一物' (wèi jié yī wù) is another rhetorical question, emphasizing that the thieves did not steal anything.
  • 既不劫不杀不淫矣，而又戕其耳 - Since they did not steal, kill, or rape, why did they cut off her ear? '戕其耳' (qiāng qí ěr) is a strong expression of the act of cutting off the ear, which adds to the mystery of the thieves' motives.
  • 既戕其耳矣，而又赠以良药 - Having cut off her ear, they also gave her good medicine. '赠以良药' (zèng yǐ liáng yào) indicates that the thieves provided a beneficial treatment, which adds a layer of complexity to their actions.
  • 是专为取耳来也？ - Was this specifically to take her ear? '专为取耳来也' (zhuān wéi qǔ ěr lái yě) is a rhetorical question, emphasizing the unusual nature of the act.
  • 取此耳又何意耶？ - What was the purpose of taking this ear? '取此耳又何意耶' (qǔ cǐ ěr yòu hé yì yé) is another rhetorical question, highlighting the mystery surrounding the thieves' motives.
  • 千思万索，终不得其所以然 - After much thought and consideration, I still cannot understand the reason. '千思万索' (qiān sī wàn suǒ) means 'to think and search in all directions,' indicating a thorough but unsuccessful attempt to understand the situation.
  • 天下真有理外事也 - Indeed, there are truly events beyond reason in the world. '理外事' (lǐ wài shì) refers to events that defy logical explanation, which adds a philosophical dimension to the story.
  • 邱生曰：苟得此盗，自必有其所以然，其所以然亦必在理中，但定非我所见之理耳 - Qiu said: If I could catch these thieves, I would certainly understand their motives. Their motives must also be within reason, but they are certainly not the kind of reason I can understand. '苟得此盗' (gǒu dé cǐ dào) means 'if I could catch these thieves,' and '其所以然亦必在理中' (qí suǒ yǐ rán yì bì zài lǐ zhōng) emphasizes that the motives are rational, even if they are not immediately apparent.
  • 然则论天下事，可据理以断有无哉！ - Therefore, when discussing matters of the world, can we rely on reason to determine their existence or non-existence? '据理以断有无' (jù lǐ yǐ duàn yǒu wú) means 'to judge existence or non-existence based on reason,' which raises a philosophical question about the limits of reason.
  • 恒兰台曰：此或采生折割之党，取以炼药，似乃近之 - Heng Lantai said: Perhaps these were members of a group that captures and mutilates people to make medicine. This seems to be the closest explanation. '采生折割之党' (cǎi shēng zhé gē zhī dǎng) refers to a group involved in capturing and mutilating people for medicinal purposes, which adds a dark and mysterious element to the story.

It highlights the superficial and rigid practices of the time.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 汝两曾伯祖 - Refers to 'your two great-uncles,' a respectful way to address ancestors.
  • 一讳景星，一讳景辰 - 'One named Jingxing, the other named Jingchen,' where '讳' (huì) is a respectful way to refer to a deceased person's name.
  • 皆名诸生也 - 'Both were famous scholars,' where '诸生' (zhū shēng) refers to students or scholars in ancient times.
  • 方在门外束幞被 - 'Were just tying up their bundles of clothes and bedding,' where '束幞被' (shù fú bèi) is a phrase describing the act of packing for travel.
  • 闻之与辩曰 - 'Hearing it, they debated and said,' where '闻之' (wén zhī) means 'hearing it' and '辩曰' (biàn yuē) means 'debated and said.'
  • 此神荼郁垒象，非尉迟敬德秦琼也 - 'These are the images of Shentu and Yulu, not of Wei Chi Jingde and Qin Qiong,' where '神荼郁垒' (shén tú yù lǜ) are mythological figures, and '尉迟敬德秦琼' (wèi chí jìng dé qín qióng) are historical figures.
  • 叟不服 - 'The old man did not accept it,' where '叟' (sǒu) refers to an old man.
  • 检丘处机西游记为证 - 'Checked the *Xiyu Ji* by Qiu Chuji as evidence,' where '西游记' (xī yóu jì) is a historical record, not to be confused with the famous novel *Journey to the West*.
  • 委巷小说 - 'Novels from the streets,' referring to popular, less reputable literature.
  • 东方朔神异经 - 'The *Shen Yi Jing* by Dongfang Shuo,' an ancient text attributed to the Han dynasty scholar Dongfang Shuo.
  • 反覆讲论又移时 - 'Repeatedly debated and discussed for a long time,' where '反覆' (fǎn fù) means 'repeatedly' and '讲论' (jiǎng lùn) means 'debate and discuss.'
  • 城门已阖 - 'The city gate had already closed,' where '阖' (hé) means 'closed.'
  • 遂不能出 - 'So they could not leave,' where '遂' (suì) means 'thus' or 'so.'
  • 次日将行 - 'The next day, they were about to leave,' where '将行' (jiāng xíng) means 'about to leave.'
  • 大兵已合围矣 - 'The large army had already surrounded the city,' where '合围' (hé wéi) means 'surrounded.'
  • 城破 - 'The city was captured,' where '破' (pò) means 'captured' or 'fallen.'
  • 遂全家遇难 - 'Thus the whole family met with disaster,' where '遇难' (nán yù) means 'met with disaster.'
  • 惟汝曾祖光禄公，曾伯祖镇番公，及叔祖云台公存耳 - 'Only your great-grandfather Guanglu Gong, your great-uncle Zhenfan Gong, and your uncle Yuntai Gong survived,' where '光禄公' (guāng lù gōng), '镇番公' (zhèn fān gōng), and '云台公' (yún tái gōng) are titles or names of ancestors.
  • 死生呼吸 - 'Life and death were in the balance,' where '死生' (sǐ shēng) means 'life and death' and '呼吸' (hū xī) means 'in the balance.'
  • 间不容发 - 'A hair's breadth apart,' emphasizing the critical nature of the moment.
  • 尚考证古书之真伪 - 'Still debated the authenticity of ancient texts,' where '考证' (kǎo zhèng) means 'to verify' and '真伪' (zhēn wěi) means 'authenticity and falsehood.'
  • 岂非惟知读书，不预外事之故哉 - 'Is this not because they only knew how to read books and did not concern themselves with external affairs?' where '岂非' (qǐ fēi) means 'is this not' and '不预外事' (bù yù wài shì) means 'did not concern themselves with external affairs.'
  • 姚安公此论 - 'This statement by Yao'an Gong,' where '姚安公' (yáo ān gōng) is a title or name of a person.
  • 余初作各种笔记，皆未敢载 - 'I initially wrote various notes but dared not include them,' where '未敢载' (wèi gǎn zài) means 'dared not include.'
  • 为涉及两曾伯祖也 - 'Because it involved your two great-uncles,' where '涉及' (shè jí) means 'involved.'
  • 书痴尚非不佳事 - 'Being a bookworm is not a bad thing,' where '书痴' (shū chī) means 'bookworm' and '尚非不佳事' (shàng fēi bù jiā shì) means 'is not a bad thing.'
  • 古来大儒似此者不一 - 'There have been many great scholars like this in ancient times,' where '大儒' (dà rú) means 'great scholar.'
  • 因补书于此 - 'Therefore, I added this here,' where '补书' (bǔ shū) means 'added' and '于此' (yú cǐ) means 'here.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 汝两曾伯祖 - Refers to the speaker's great-uncles, emphasizing family lineage and respect for ancestors.
  • 一讳景星，一讳景辰 - Names of the great-uncles, with '讳' (huì) indicating respect for their names.
  • 皆名诸生也 - Scholars or students in ancient China, reflecting the value placed on education.
  • 神荼郁垒 - Mythological figures believed to guard the gates of hell, often depicted on doorways for protection.
  • 尉迟敬德秦琼 - Historical figures, Wei Chi Jingde and Qin Qiong, who were famous generals and protectors in Chinese history.
  • 丘处机西游记 - A historical record by Qiu Chuji, a Taoist master, not to be confused with the famous novel *Journey to the West*.
  • 委巷小说 - Popular, less reputable literature, often dismissed by scholars.
  • 东方朔神异经 - An ancient text attributed to Dongfang Shuo, a famous scholar and court jester during the Han dynasty.
  • 姚安公 - A title or name of a person, likely a respected figure in the speaker's family or community.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening sentence introduces the two great-uncles and their status as scholars, setting the context for the story.
  • This clause describes the action of the great-uncles just before the incident, creating a vivid scene.
  • The debate centers on the identity of the figures depicted, with the great-uncles arguing against the old man's interpretation.
  • The old man's reaction and his attempt to prove his point using a historical record.
  • The great-uncles' response, dismissing the old man's evidence and bringing in a more authoritative text.
  • The prolonged debate and its consequences, leading to the great-uncles being trapped.
  • The next day's events, with the great-uncles about to leave but finding the city already surrounded.
  • The tragic outcome, with the city falling and the family meeting disaster.
  • The few survivors, emphasizing the loss and the importance of the remaining family members.
  • The critical moment and the great-uncles' focus on scholarly debate, highlighting their dedication to learning.

  • 无所用其技 - Has no use for his skills, indicating that the slave's talents are not being utilized.
  • 颇郁郁不自得 - Quite unhappy and discontented, describing the slave's emotional state.
  • 年八十余尚健饭 - In his eighties, he is still strong and can eat well, indicating the slave's physical condition.
  • 时一携鸟铳，散步野外而已 - Occasionally he carries a bird gun and walks in the fields, describing the slave's daily activities.
  • 铳发无不中 - The gun never misses its target, highlighting the slave's skill with the gun.
  • 狐亦不惊 - The foxes are not alarmed, indicating the foxes' lack of fear.

  • 后亦无他 - Thereafter, nothing else happened, indicating that no further incidents occurred.
  • 外祖张公水明楼有值更者范玉夜 - My maternal grandfather, Zhang Gong, at the Shuiming Tower, had a night watchman named Fan Yu. This introduces the setting and characters.
  • 每闻瓦上有声，疑为盗 - He often hears sounds on the roof tiles and suspects thieves, describing the watchman's concerns.
  • 起视则无有，潜踪侦之 - He gets up to look but sees nothing, then stealthily investigates, describing the watchman's actions.
  • 见一黑影从屋上过 - He sees a black shadow pass over the roof, describing the sighting.
  • 乃设机瓦沟，仰卧以听 - He sets up a trap in the roof tiles and lies on his back to listen, describing the watchman's preparations.
  • 半夜闻机发，有女子呼痛声 - In the middle of the night, he hears the trap go off and a woman's cry of pain, describing the incident.
  • 登屋寻视，一黑狐折股死矣 - He climbs onto the roof to investigate and finds a black fox with a broken leg dead, describing the outcome.
  • 是夕闻屋上詈曰：范玉何故杀我妾 - That night, he hears someone cursing from the roof: 'Why did you kill my concubine, Fan Yu?' describing the supernatural response.
  • 时邻有刘氏子为妖所媚 - At that time, a neighbor's son was bewitched by a demon, providing background information.
  • 私度必是狐 - He privately thinks it must be that fox, indicating the watchman's suspicion.
  • 亦还詈曰：汝纵妾私奔，不知自愧，反詈吾，吾为刘氏子除患也 - He also curses back: 'You let your concubine run off, not knowing shame, and you blame me. I am doing the neighbor's son a favor by killing you.' describing the watchman's response.
  • 遂寂无语 - Then there is silence, indicating the end of the interaction.
  • 然自是觉夜夜有人以石灰渗其目，交睫即来，旋洗拭旋又如是，渐肿痛溃裂，竟至双瞽 - But from then on, he feels that every night someone is pouring lime into his eyes, and as soon as he closes his eyes, it happens again. He washes it off but it happens again, gradually causing swelling, pain, and ulcers, eventually leading to blindness, describing the watchman's suffering.
  • 盖狐之报也 - This is the fox's revenge, explaining the cause of the watchman's blindness.
  • 其所见逊刘福荣远矣 - What he saw is far inferior to what Liu Furing saw, comparing the watchman's experience to the slave's.
  • 一老成经事，一少年喜事故也 - One is an experienced adult, and the other is a young man who enjoys adventures, summarizing the difference between the two characters.

  • 刘氏子 - Refers to a neighbor's son who is bewitched by a demon.

[1252]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 汪閣學曉園言 - Classical introductory phrase meaning 'Master Wang, the scholar of the Hanlin Academy (or Grand Secretary), Xiu Yuan, said.' Sets up the narrative as a report from a learned official.
  • 泫然流涕 - Classical expression meaning 'tears streaming down the face' or 'shedding tears,' used to describe emotional distress.
  • 或讶之 - 'Someone was surprised by it,' a common way to introduce a reaction to an unusual event.
  • 吾能记两世事 - 'I can remember two lifetimes' worth of events,' a clear statement of the speaker's claim to have lived two lives.
  • 冥官责以杀业至重 - 'The underworld officials blamed me for the heavy sin of killing,' a reference to karmic judgment in Buddhist or Daoist cosmology.
  • 押赴转轮受恶报 - 'They took me to the Wheel of Rebirth to receive a bad karmic consequence,' referring to the Buddhist concept of samsara and karmic retribution.
  • 惟恼热不可忍 - 'Only the burning heat was unbearable,' a description of the torment in hell.
  • 忽似清凉 - 'Suddenly it seemed cool,' indicating a shift in the karmic state.
  • 已在豕栏矣 - 'I was already in the pigpen,' marking the rebirth as a pig.
  • 断乳后见食不洁 - 'After weaning, I saw the food was unclean,' indicating the pig's awareness of the filth of its diet.
  • 心知其秽 - 'I knew it was filthy,' showing the pig's consciousness.
  • 饥火燔烧 - 'The fire of hunger burned,' a metaphor for intense hunger.
  • 五脏皆如焦裂 - 'All five internal organs felt like they were burned to pieces,' a hyperbolic expression of suffering.
  • 不得已食之 - 'I had no choice but to eat it,' indicating necessity despite distaste.
  • 后渐通猪语 - 'Later, I gradually understood pig language,' indicating the pig's growing awareness of its species.
  • 时与同类相问讯 - 'I would sometimes greet my fellow pigs,' showing social interaction.
  • 能记前身者颇多 - 'Many could remember their previous lives,' indicating that other pigs also had memories of their past.
  • 特不能与人言耳 - 'But they could not speak to humans,' a limitation on communication.
  • 大抵皆自知当屠割 - 'Most of them knew they were to be slaughtered,' showing the pigs' awareness of their fate.
  • 其时作呻吟声者愁也 - 'Those who made moaning sounds were in sorrow,' a description of emotional suffering.
  • 目睫往往有湿痕者，自悲也 - 'Those whose eyelashes often had wet streaks were self-pitying,' indicating emotional distress.
  • 躯干痴重 - 'Their bodies were dull and heavy,' a description of physical condition.
  • 夏极苦热 - 'In summer, they suffered greatly from the heat,' indicating discomfort.
  • 惟汨没泥水中少可 - 'Only by submerging in mud water was it somewhat bearable,' a description of the pig's relief.
  • 然不常得 - 'But it was not often possible,' indicating the rarity of such relief.
  • 毛悚而劲 - 'Their fur was bristling and strong,' describing the physical state of the pig.
  • 冬极苦寒 - 'In winter, they suffered greatly from the cold,' indicating another form of discomfort.
  • 视犬羊软毳厚，有如仙兽 - 'Seeing dogs and sheep with soft, thick fur, they seemed like divine beasts,' a comparison highlighting the pigs' discomfort and the superior comfort of other animals.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 汪阁学晓园 - Refers to Wang Xiu Yuan, a scholar who held the title of Ge Xue (翰林院学士), a high-ranking academic position in the imperial court, often associated with the Hanlin Academy.
  • 冥官 - 'Underworld officials,' referring to the bureaucratic system of the afterlife in Chinese cosmology, often associated with Buddhist or Daoist beliefs about karma and reincarnation.
  • 转轮 - 'Wheel of Rebirth,' a Buddhist concept of samsara, the cycle of birth, death, and rebirth, driven by karma.
  • 豕栏 - 'Pigpen,' a literal reference to the place where the soul is reborn as a pig.
  • 杀业 - 'Killing karma,' a Buddhist concept referring to the karmic consequences of killing.
  • 恶报 - 'Bad karmic consequence,' a term from Buddhist and Daoist teachings.
  • 豕 - 'Pig,' a common animal in Chinese cosmology and rebirth narratives.
  • 断乳 - 'Weaning,' a biological milestone in the life of a pig, used here to mark the beginning of the pig's awareness of its condition.
  • 食不洁 - 'Eating unclean food,' a reference to the pig's instinctive aversion to filth, which is ironic in the context of its rebirth as a pig.
  • 猪语 - 'Pig language,' a metaphor for the communication among pigs, suggesting a form of consciousness or language.
  • 问讯 - 'Greeting or exchanging news,' a common term in classical Chinese for social interaction.
  • 前身 - 'Previous life,' a term from Buddhist and Daoist cosmology.
  • 呻吟声 - 'Moaning sounds,' used to describe the emotional and physical suffering of the pigs.
  • 湿痕 - 'Wet streaks,' a metaphor for tears or signs of sorrow.
  • 自悲 - 'Self-pity,' a term indicating emotional suffering.
  • 痴重 - 'Dull and heavy,' a term used to describe a slow, sluggish physical condition.
  • 汨没泥水中 - 'Submerged in mud water,' a description of a pig's natural behavior and a form of temporary relief from heat.
  • 毛悚而劲 - 'Bristling and strong fur,' describing the physical appearance of the pig.
  • 犬羊 - 'Dogs and sheep,' animals that are often contrasted with pigs in classical Chinese texts, either for their cleanliness or their domestication.
  • 软毳厚 - 'Soft, downy, thick,' describing the fur of other animals.
  • 仙兽 - 'Divine beasts,' a term used to describe animals that are seen as noble or sacred in Chinese cosmology.

[1253]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 遇捕執時 - Means 'when captured and seized,' describing the moment of apprehension.
  • 姑跳踉奔避 - Literally 'temporarily leap and run to avoid,' indicating a desperate attempt to escape.
  • 冀緩須臾 - Means 'hoping to delay for a moment,' expressing the fleeting hope of escape.
  • 追得后蹴踏头项 - Literally 'after being caught, being kicked and stepped on the head and neck,' describing the brutal treatment after capture.
  • 拗捩蹄肘 - Means 'twisting and bending the hooves and elbows,' indicating physical torture.
  • 绳勒四足深至骨 - Literally 'ropes binding the four legs deeply to the bone,' describing severe restraint.
  • 痛若刀眘 - Means 'pain as if being cut by a knife,' emphasizing the intensity of the pain.
  • 或载以舟车 - Means 'or carried on boats and carts,' indicating transportation.
  • 重叠相压 - Literally 'stacked and pressed on each other,' describing the weight and pressure.
  • 肋如欲折 - Means 'ribs as if about to break,' indicating extreme physical strain.
  • 百脉涌塞 - Literally 'all veins blocked,' describing a state of physical distress.
  • 腹如欲裂 - Means 'abdomen as if about to split,' indicating severe abdominal pain.
  • 或贯以竿而扛之 - Means 'or carried on poles,' describing another method of transportation.
  • 更痛甚三木矣 - Literally 'more painful than the three wooden devices,' referring to a form of torture.
  • 至屠市提掷于地 - Means 'to the slaughterhouse, thrown to the ground,' describing the final destination and treatment.
  • 心脾皆震动欲碎 - Literally 'heart and spleen all shaken to the point of breaking,' indicating extreme emotional and physical distress.
  • 或即日死 - Means 'or die on the same day,' indicating the possibility of immediate death.
  • 或缚至数日 - Means 'or bound for several days,' indicating prolonged suffering.
  • 弥难忍受 - Means 'even more difficult to endure,' emphasizing the increasing difficulty of the suffering.
  • 时见刀俎在左 - Means 'often seeing the knife and cutting board to the left,' describing the presence of tools of slaughter.
  • 汤镬在右 - Refers to 'boiling pot to the right,' describing another tool of torture or execution.
  • 不知著我身时 - Means 'not knowing when it will happen to me,' indicating fear and uncertainty.
  • 辄簌簌战栗不止 - Means 'constantly trembling uncontrollably,' describing the physical reaction to fear.
  • 又时自顾己身 - Means 'also often looking at oneself,' indicating self-awareness.
  • 念将来不知磔裂分散 - Means 'thinking about the future, not knowing if it will be torn apart and scattered,' indicating fear of a gruesome fate.
  • 作谁家杯中羹 - Means 'become someone else's soup,' indicating a dehumanizing fate.
  • 凄惨欲绝 - Means 'extremely sad and pitiful,' describing the overall emotional state.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 三木 - Refers to a form of torture involving wooden devices, often used in ancient Chinese punishments.
  • 刀俎 - Refers to the knife and cutting board, commonly used in butchery and symbolizing the act of slaughter.
  • 汤镬 - Refers to a boiling pot, often used in ancient Chinese executions by boiling alive.
  • 磔裂分散 - Refers to the act of being torn apart and scattered, a form of execution in ancient China.
  • 杯中羹 - Refers to soup, often used metaphorically to describe a dehumanizing fate where one's body is reduced to food.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening sequence '遇捕執時，自知不免，姑跳踉奔避，冀缓须臾' describes the moment of capture and the desperate attempt to escape.
  • The sequence '追得后蹴踏头项，拗捩蹄肘，绳勒四足深至骨，痛若刀眘' describes the brutal treatment after capture.
  • The sequence '或载以舟车，则重叠相压，肋如欲折，百脉涌塞，腹如欲裂' describes the physical strain during transportation.
  • The sequence '或贯以竿而扛之，更痛甚三木矣' describes another method of transportation and the associated pain.
  • The sequence '至屠市提掷于地，心脾皆震动欲碎，或即日死，或缚至数日，弥难忍受' describes the final destination and the increasing difficulty of the suffering.
  • The sequence '时见刀俎在左，汤镬在右，不知著我身时，作何痛楚，辄簌簌战栗不止' describes the fear and uncertainty of the impending fate.
  • The sequence '又时自顾己身，念将来不知磔裂分散，作谁家杯中羹，凄惨欲绝' describes the self-awareness and fear of a gruesome fate.

  • 千字文 - A classic educational text used for teaching literacy in ancient China.
  • 姚安公 - Likely a title or nickname for a respected elder in the family.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This phrase is elliptical but means 'spirits occupy the house, and people rarely go there.'
  • This phrase is also elliptical but means 'occasionally a guest might stay, but only temporarily.'
  • This long sentence can be broken down into a series of logical steps: 'If not, since no one lives there, the house will not be maintained, and over time it will collapse on its own, where will you go then?'
  • This long sentence describes the background of the servant Xi, emphasizing his slow progress in learning.
  • This phrase is elliptical but means 'however, he is naturally loyal and straightforward, treating the master's affairs as his own, even if they are disliked or resented.'

Proper Nouns

  • 施祥 - The name of the servant who speaks about ghosts.
  • 刘文斗 - The name of another servant who comments on the first servant's statement.
  • 姚安公 - A respected elder in the family.
  • 太夫人 - A respected lady, likely the mother or grandmother of the author.
  • 四官 - A nickname or title for the author or a family member.
  • 廖媪 - The name of an elderly woman who is trusted and relied upon in the household.

[1268]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 先兄晴湖第四子汝来 - Standard way of introducing a family member in classical Chinese.
  • 幼韶秀 - Archaic expression meaning 'graceful' or 'handsome,' often used to describe youth.
  • 亦颇知读书 - Common phrase for knowing how to read or being fond of reading.
  • 忽患颠狂 - Classical term for madness or insanity, implying a sudden and uncontrollable mental state.
  • 如无人料理 - '料理' here is used in the sense of 'to manage' or 'to care for.'
  • 即发不理，面不盥 - Combination implies neglect of personal hygiene.
  • 夏或衣絮，冬或衣葛 - Specific materials (flax and hemp), and the sentence reflects an unusual or inappropriate choice of clothing for the season.
  • 不自知也 - Standard way of expressing lack of self-awareness.
  • 呼之食即食，不呼之食亦不索 - Typical classical structure for contrasting actions based on external stimuli.
  • 或自取市中饼饵，呼儿童共食 - '饼饵' is a general term for baked goods or bread.
  • 不问其价，所残剩亦不顾惜 - '不顾惜' implies a lack of concern or attachment.
  • 或一两日觅之不得，忽自归 - '觅之不得' is a common expression for being unable to find someone.
  • 一日遍索无迹 - '遍索' means to search thoroughly.
  • 或云村外柳林内似仿佛有人 - '似仿佛' is a classical expression for 'seemed as if' or 'appeared as though.'
  • 趋视，已端坐僵矣 - '端坐僵' suggests a lifeless or motionless state, possibly indicating death.
  • 其为迷惑而死，未可知也 - '迷惑' can mean confusion, bewilderment, or even a state of spiritual or mental disarray.
  • 其或自有所得，托以混迹，缘尽而化去，亦未可知也 - '托以混迹' implies using a state of madness as a means to avoid notice or to live in obscurity. '缘尽而化去' is a classical expression suggesting the end of a karmic connection or spiritual journey.
  • 忆余从福建归里时，见余犹跪拜如礼 - '跪拜如礼' is a formal expression of respectful bowing.
  • 拜讫，卒然曰：叔大辛苦 - '卒然' means 'suddenly' or 'unexpectedly.'
  • 余曰：是无奈何 - '无奈何' is a common expression for 'there is no help for it' or 'it cannot be avoided.'
  • 又卒然曰：叔不觉辛苦耶？ - '不觉辛苦耶' is a rhetorical question asking whether the listener is aware of the difficulty.
  • 默默退去 - '默默' means 'silently' or 'quietly,' and '退去' is to withdraw or leave.
  • 后思其言，似若有意，故至今终莫能测之 - '似若有意' is a classical expression suggesting an underlying purpose or meaning.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 先兄晴湖 - Personal name; *晴湖* is likely a nickname or a given name, not a title or historical figure.
  • 福建 - Name of a province in China.
  • 跪拜如礼 - Reflects the Confucian emphasis on proper etiquette and filial piety in classical Chinese society.
  • 缘尽而化去 - Phrase with Buddhist and Daoist connotations, suggesting the end of a karmic or spiritual connection, and the transformation or disappearance of a being.
  • 托以混迹 - Implies a philosophical or spiritual stance, where one uses a state of madness or eccentricity as a way to live in the world without being noticed or disturbed.

Proper Nouns

  • 先兄晴湖 - Personal name; *晴湖* is a personal name, not a title or historical figure.
  • 福建 - Name of a Chinese province.
  • 汝来 - The name of the person being described, likely a personal name.

[1270]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 好以香奁艳体，寓不遇之感 - Poetic expression meaning 'likes to use perfumed cases and ornate styles to express feelings of unfulfilled aspirations or misfortune.'
  • 尝谒某公未见 - 'Once visited a certain official but was not received.'
  • 戏为无题诗曰 - 'Wrote a playful poem without a title, saying...'
  • 垩粉围墙罨画楼 - Poetic description of a white-painted wall and a painted pavilion, evoking a scenic or romantic setting.
  • 分无信使通青鸟 - 'No messenger can deliver messages through the green bird (a mythological bird that conveys messages, often associated with the Queen of Heaven).'
  • 枉遣游人驻紫骝 - 'In vain did the traveler stop his purple steed (a noble horse).'
  • 月姊定应随顾兔 - 'The moon goddess (月姊) must surely follow the moon rabbit (顾兔, a mythological creature associated with the moon).'
  • 星娥可止待牵牛 - 'The star maiden (星娥) may stop and wait for the oxherd (牵牛, a mythological figure associated with the Cowherd and Weaver Girl legend).'
  • 垂杨疏处雕栊近 - 'In the sparse branches of willows, the carved window is near.'
  • 只恨珠帘不上钩 - 'Only regret that the pearl curtain does not hook (i.e., cannot be drawn up).'
  • 殊有玉溪生风致 - 'It has the style of Yuxi Sheng (李商隐), the pen name of Li Shangyin, a famous Tang poet.'
  • 王近光曰 - 'Wang Jingguang said...'
  • 似不应疑及织女，诬蔑仙灵 - 'It seems one should not suspect the Weaving Girl (织女), accusing her of being a false or impious spirit.'
  • 余曰：已矣哉 - 'I say: Alas, it is over...'
  • 织女别黄姑，一年一度一相见 - 'The Weaving Girl separates from the Cowherd (黄姑), meeting once a year.'
  • 彼此隔河何事无 - 'Between them, separated by a river, what things are not there?'
  • 元微之诗也 - 'This is a poem by Yuan Weizhi (元稹), a Tang poet.'
  • 海客乘槎上紫氛 - 'A sea traveler rides a raft into the purple haze (a reference to a mythological journey to the celestial realms).'
  • 星娥罢织一相闻 - 'The star maiden stops weaving and meets him once.'
  • 只应不惮牵牛妒 - 'Only because she does not fear the jealousy of the oxherd.'
  • 故把支机石赠君 - 'Therefore, she gives you the stone of the loom (支机石, a stone from the Weaving Girl's loom).'
  • 李义山诗也 - 'This is a poem by Li Yishan (李义山), the pen name of Li Shangyin.'
  • 微之之意，在于双文 - 'Yuan Weizhi's intention was toward Shuang Wen (a historical or literary figure).'
  • 义山之意，在于令狐 - 'Li Yishan's intention was toward Linghu (令狐, a family name of a political figure).'
  • 文士掉弄笔墨，借为比喻 - 'Literary men play with ink and brush, using them as metaphors.'
  • 初与织女无涉 - 'Originally, they had no connection to the Weaving Girl.'
  • 铁蟾此语，亦犹元李之志云尔 - 'Iron Toad’s words are similar to the intentions of Yuan and Li.'
  • 未为诬蔑仙灵也 - 'Thus, it is not defaming the celestial spirits.'
  • 至于纯构虚词，宛如实事 - 'As for purely constructing fictitious words, making them seem like real events.'
  • 指其时地，撰以姓名 - 'Pointing to a time and place, inventing names.'
  • 灵怪集所载郭翰遇织女事 - 'The story of Guo Han meeting the Weaving Girl, as recorded in *Lingguai Ji* (灵怪集), a collection of strange tales.'
  • 灵怪集今佚，此条见太平广记六十八 - '*Lingguai Ji* is now lost; this entry appears in *Taiping Guangji* (太平广记), volume 68.'
  • 悖妄之甚矣 - 'This is extremely absurd and baseless.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太原申铁蟾 - A historical or literary figure from the Tang or later periods, known for writing poetry with romantic and emotional themes.

  • 顾兔 - A mythological rabbit on the moon, associated with the moon goddess and the legend of the moon.
  • 星娥 - A poetic term for the star maiden, often associated with the Weaving Girl (织女) in the Cowherd and Weaver Girl legend.
  • 牵牛 - The Cowherd, a mythological figure in the legend of the Weaving Girl and the Cowherd, who is separated from her by the Milky Way.
  • 黄姑 - A poetic or colloquial term for the Cowherd, often used in classical poetry.
  • 双文 - Likely refers to a female figure named Shuang Wen, possibly a historical or literary character.
  • 令狐 - A family name associated with the Tang dynasty politician and poet Linghu Du (令狐绹), who was a patron of both Yuan and Li.

Proper Nouns

  • 太原申铁蟾 - A proper name, likely referring to a historical figure or literary character from the Tang or later periods. *太原* is the place name *Taiyuan*, and *申铁蟾* is the personal name.

  • 龙种由来事杳冥 - Means 'the dragon offspring has always been a mysterious matter,' reinforcing the idea of Liu Bang's divine origin.

  • 学者当考校真妄 - Means 'scholars should examine truth and falsehood,' emphasizing the importance of critical thinking.
  • 炫博矜奇 - Means 'to show off knowledge and seek novelty,' criticizing the tendency to overemphasize obscure or sensational information.

[1272]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 從叔梅庵公 - Refers to the author's uncle, who is the narrator of the story.
  • 聞某墓中有狐迹 - Colloquial way of saying that there were signs of fox activity in a certain tomb.
  • 夜携铳往 - Direct and literal translation, meaning 'at night, they took a gun and went'.
  • 共伏草中伺之 - Describes their actions in a straightforward manner, meaning 'they both hid in the grass and waited for it'.
  • 以背相倚而睡 - Vivid description of their position, meaning 'they slept with their backs leaning against each other'.
  • 醒则两人之发交结为一，贯穿缭绕 - Detailed and somewhat poetic description of their entanglement, meaning 'when they woke up, their hair was intertwined into one, passing through and winding around each other'.
  • 猝不可解 - Indicates the suddenness and difficulty of the situation, meaning 'suddenly, they could not be untangled'.
  • 互相牵掣不能行，亦不能立 - Describes the physical struggle, meaning 'they were pulling each other and could neither move nor stand'.
  • 稍稍转动，即彼此呼痛 - Vivid depiction of their discomfort, meaning 'slightly moving, they both cried out in pain'.
  • 胶扰彻晓 - Emphasizes the duration of their entanglement, meaning 'they were stuck together all night'.
  • 望见行路者，始呼至，断以佩刀 - Clear sequence of events, meaning 'seeing a passerby, they called for help, and the passerby cut them apart with a dagger'.
  • 愤欲往报 - Expresses their initial reaction, meaning 'they were angry and wanted to go back to report'.
  • 父老曰 - Introduces the advice given by the older generation, meaning 'the elders said'.
  • 彼无形声，非力所胜 - Metaphorical description of the foxes' nature, meaning 'they have no form or sound, and cannot be overcome by force'.
  • 且无故而侵彼，理亦不直 - Moral argument, meaning 'moreover, without reason to invade them, the logic is not right'.
  • 侮实自召，又何仇焉 - Rhetorical question emphasizing the folly of retaliation, meaning 'the insult was self-invited, so why harbor resentment?'.
  • 仇必败滋甚 - Warning about the consequences of revenge, meaning 'retaliation will only lead to greater failure'.
  • 二人乃止 - Indicates the resolution of the conflict, meaning 'the two then stopped'.
  • 此狐小虐之使警 - Explains the purpose of the foxes' actions, meaning 'this was a small torment to make them cautious'.
  • 不深创之，以激其必报 - Strategic consideration, meaning 'without deeply harming them, to provoke a certain revenge'.
  • 亦可谓善自全矣 - Final evaluation, meaning 'it can also be said that they were good at preserving themselves'.
  • 然小虐亦足以激怒 - Counterpoint to the previous evaluation, meaning 'however, even a small torment can provoke anger'.
  • 不如敛戢勿动，使伺之无迹弥善也 - Concluding piece of advice, meaning 'it is better to restrain oneself and not act, making sure there are no traces to be sought even better'.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 从叔梅庵公 - The author's uncle, who is the narrator of the story.
  • 狐迹 - Fox tracks, which in Chinese folklore often symbolize supernatural or magical elements.
  • 铳 - A firearm, indicating a time period when firearms were in use.
  • 佩刀 - A dagger, a common weapon in historical Chinese narratives.
  • 父老 - Elders or wise old men, often respected figures in traditional Chinese society.

Proper Nouns

  • 从叔梅庵公 - The narrator, the author's uncle.
  • 铳 - A firearm.
  • 佩刀 - A dagger.

  • 中彼卷之开花临上界，持斧有仙郎，何如中此诗乎 - How does the poem in the other scroll, which speaks of flowers blooming in the upper realm and a celestial youth holding an axe, compare to this poem?
  • 微公拨入，亦自愿易之 - If it were not for your intervention, I would have willingly changed it, indicating a voluntary change due to external influence.
  • 余丙子扈从古北口时，车马拥塞，就旅舍小憩，见壁上一诗，剥残过半，惟三四句可辨，最爱其一水涨喧人语外，万山青到马蹄前二语 - During the year of Bingzi, when I was accompanying the emperor to the north of the Great Wall, the carriages and horses were congested. I took a rest in a hostel and saw a poem on the wall, half of which was worn away, but only a few lines were still legible. I loved the lines 'The water rises, and the human voices grow distant; the green mountains stretch to the hooves of the horses.'
  • 以为云中路绕巴山色，树里河流汉水声，不是过也 - I thought that 'The road in the clouds winds around the color of Bashan, and the trees hide the sound of the Han River flowing,' was not an exaggeration.
  • 惜不得姓名，及展其卷，此诗在焉，乃知芥契合，已在六七年前，相与叹息者久之 - I regretted not knowing the author's name, but when I opened his scroll, this poem was there. Thus, I realized that our affinity had been present for six or seven years, and we sighed together for a long time.
  • 子颖待余最尽礼，殁后，其二子承父之志，见余尚依依有情 - Zhu Ziyi treated me with the utmost courtesy, and after his death, his two sons continued his legacy, and I still felt a deep affection for them.
  • 翰墨因缘，良非偶尔 - The connection through writing and calligraphy was indeed not accidental.
  • 何尝以拨房为亲疏哉 - How could I ever consider the selection of rooms as a measure of closeness or distance? This is a rhetorical question emphasizing the importance of content over form.
  • 余严江舟中诗曰：山色空濛淡似烟，参差绿到大江边，斜阳流水推篷坐，处处随人欲上船 - My poem on the boat in the Yangtze River says: 'The mountain colors are hazy and light as smoke, the uneven green reaches the edge of the great river, the setting sun and flowing water urge me to sit under the awning, and everywhere I go, I want to board a boat.'
  • 实从万山句夺胎 - This is indeed derived from the line 'The green mountains stretch to the hooves of the horses.'
  • 尝以语子颖曰：人言青出于蓝，今日乃蓝出于青 - I once told Zhu Ziyi: 'People say that blue comes from indigo, but today it is the indigo that comes from the blue.'
  • 子颖虽逊谢，意似默可 - Zhu Ziyi humbly declined, but his expression seemed to silently agree.
  • 此亦诗坛之佳话，并附录于此 - This is also a fine story in the poetry world, and I will record it here.

[1285]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 门人 - Refers to a student or disciple, often used in the context of Confucian scholars.
  • 寓言滑稽 - Means 'allegorical humor,' indicating that the text uses humor to convey deeper meanings.
  • 以文为戏 - Means 'using writing as a form of play or entertainment.'
  • 值金川之役 - Means 'during the Jinchuan campaign,' referring to a military conflict during the Qing dynasty.
  • 以监运火药殁于路 - Means 'died on the road while supervising the transport of gunpowder.'
  • 诗文皆散佚 - Means 'poetry and prose have all been lost,' indicating that the original works are no longer available.
  • 惟此篇偶得于故纸中 - Means 'only this piece was accidentally found among old papers,' suggesting that the text was discovered in an unexpected place.
  • 其词曰 - Means 'the text says,' introducing the content of the discovered piece.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 门人吴钟侨 - Refers to Wu Zhongqiao, a student of the author, who wrote a humorous allegory.
  • 金川之役 - Refers to the Jinchuan Campaign, a series of military expeditions against the Dzungar Khanate in the late 18th century.
  • 监运火药 - Refers to the role of supervising the transportation of gunpowder, a critical task during military campaigns.
  • 水府 - Refers to the realm of water gods or spirits, a common theme in Chinese mythology.
  • 彭泽清洪君 - Refers to a legendary figure in Chinese folklore, often associated with water deities.
  • 庐陵欧明 - Refers to Ou Ming, a historical or legendary figure from Lu陵, a region in Jiangxi province.
  • 如愿 - Refers to a goddess who fulfills wishes, a concept found in Chinese folklore and literature.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 门人吴钟侨尝作如愿小传 - This phrase introduces the author and his work, but the exact nature of '如愿小传' (a short biography of the goddess of fulfillment) is not immediately clear.
  • 寓言滑稽，以文为戏也 - This clause explains the nature of the work, but the specific elements of humor and allegory need to be carefully translated to maintain the intended effect.
  • 后作蜀中一令，值金川之役，以监运火药殁于路 - This sequence describes the author's later life and death, with a focus on the specific circumstances of his demise.
  • 诗文皆散佚，惟此篇偶得于故纸中，附录于此 - This clause explains the discovery and inclusion of the text, emphasizing its accidental nature and rarity.

Proper Nouns

  • 吴钟侨 - Wu Zhongqiao, the author of the humorous allegory.
  • 金川 - Jinchuan, a region in Sichuan province.

  • 殆所謂冰蠶者欤 - Suggests that these might be what are called 'ice worms.'

  • 极阳之内必伏阴，极阴之内必伏阳 - Indicates that within extreme heat there is latent cold, and within extreme cold there is latent heat.

[1354]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 坎以二陰包一陽，離以二陽包一陰 - Describes the structure of the Kan (坎) and Li (離) hexagrams in the I Ching (易經). Kan consists of two yin lines enclosing one yang line, while Li consists of two yang lines enclosing one yin line.
  • 陽極於乾，即一陰生 - Refers to the principle of yin and yang transformation, where when yang reaches its extreme (乾, Qian), yin begins to emerge.
  • 陰極於坤，即一陽生 - Similarly, when yin reaches its extreme (坤, Kun), yang begins to emerge.
  • 其靜也伏斯斂，斂斯郁焉 - Describes the process of contraction and stagnation in a state of rest.
  • 其動也郁斯蒸，蒸斯化焉 - Describes the process of expansion and transformation in a state of motion.
  • 至於化則生，生不已矣 - Means that when transformation occurs, life emerges, and this process continues endlessly.
  • 特沖和之氣，其生有常 - Refers to the harmonious and balanced energy (沖和之氣) that produces life in a regular and predictable manner.
  • 偏勝之氣，其生不測 - Refers to the imbalanced energy (偏勝之氣) that produces life in unpredictable ways.
  • 沸鼎炎磓 - Describes a boiling cauldron with intense heat.
  • 寒泉磖結 - Describes a cold spring with frozen water.
  • 其中皆可以生蟲也 - Means that both hot and cold environments can support the growth of insects.
  • 崔豹古今注載火鼠生炎洲火中 - Refers to the Gujin Zhi (古今注) by Cui Bao, which records the existence of fire mice in the fire region (炎洲).
  • 績其毛為布，入火不燃 - Means that the fur of the fire mouse can be spun into cloth that does not burn in fire.
  • 神異經載冰鼠生北海冰中 - Refers to the Shenyi Jing (神異經), which records the existence of ice mice in the ice of the North Sea (北海).
  • 穴冰而居，齧水而食 - Means that the ice mouse lives in ice and feeds on water.
  • 歲久大如象，冰破即死 - Means that over time, the ice mouse grows as large as an elephant and dies when the ice melts.
  • 歐羅巴人曾見之 - Means that Europeans have seen the ice mouse.
  • 謝梅莊前輩戍烏里雅蘇台時，亦曾見之 - Refers to the fact that Xie Meizhuang, a senior official, saw the ice mouse during his time in Uliastai.
  • 其事似異，實則常理也 - Means that although these phenomena seem strange, they are actually consistent with natural principles.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 崔豹 - A scholar who wrote the Gujin Zhi (古今注), a historical and cultural encyclopedia.
  • 神異經 - An ancient Chinese text that records strange and supernatural phenomena.
  • 歐羅巴 - The ancient Chinese term for Europe.
  • 謝梅莊 - A historical figure who served as an official in Uliastai, a region in Inner Mongolia.

Proper Nouns

  • 坎 - The Kan trigram in the I Ching.
  • 離 - The Li trigram in the I Ching.
  • 乾 - The Qian trigram in the I Ching.
  • 坤 - The Kun trigram in the I Ching.
  • 崔豹 - The author of the Gujin Zhi.
  • 神異經 - The title of the ancient text that records strange phenomena.

[1356]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 數日後微聞 - A few days later, it was faintly heard; introduces a delayed and vague piece of information.
  • 一日遣二仆 - One day, he sent away two servants; describes a specific action taken by the owner.
  • 罪状不明 - The charges were unclear; indicates that the reasons for the servants' dismissal were not specified.
  • 旋有泄其事者曰 - Soon, someone revealed the matter, saying that...; introduces a subsequent revelation about the incident.
  • 二仆皆谋为司阍 - The two servants both planned to become gatekeepers; describes the servants' intentions.
  • 无如先已有跛者 - But there was already a lame person serving as gatekeeper; explains the obstacle to their plans.
  • 乃各因饰其妇 - So they each tried to beautify their wives; describes the servants' strategy.
  • 俟主人燕息 - Waiting for the master to rest; indicates the timing of their actions.
  • 诱而蛊之 - They lured and bewitched him; describes the servants' method of approach.
  • 至夕，一妇私具饼饵，一妇私煎茶 - By evening, one wife secretly prepared some cakes and pastries, and another wife secretly brewed tea; describes the wives' actions.
  • 皆暗中摸索至书斋廊下 - Both of them groped their way in the dark to the veranda of the study; describes the setting of the encounter.
  • 猝然相触，所赍俱倾 - Suddenly they bumped into each other, and everything they carried fell to the ground; describes the accidental collision.
  • 愧不自容，转怒而相诟 - Embarrassed and unable to bear it, they turned angry and scolded each other; describes the servants' reaction.
  • 主人不欲深究，故善遣去 - The master did not want to investigate deeply, so he kindly dismissed them; explains the master's decision.
  • 此乩可谓灵鬼矣 - This divination can be called a very clever spirit; comments on the accuracy of the divination.
  • 然何以能前知此等事，终无理可推也 - But how could it predict such things in advance? It's ultimately beyond explanation; expresses skepticism about the divination's predictive power.
  • 马夫人雇一针线人，曾在是家 - Madam Ma hired a needlewoman who had previously worked in this household; introduces a new character.
  • 初无自献其妇意 - She had no intention of offering her wife as a gift; clarifies the needlewoman's intentions.
  • 乃私谋于一黠仆 - But she privately plotted with a cunning servant; describes the needlewoman's actions.
  • 黠仆为画此策 - The cunning servant devised this plan; explains the servant's role.
  • 均与约是日有暇，可乘隙以进 - They agreed that on that day they would have free time and could take advantage of the opportunity to approach; describes the plan.
  • 而不使相知 - But they did not let each other know; explains the secrecy of the plan.
  • 故致两败 - Thus, both plans failed; summarizes the outcome.
  • 二仆逐后，黠仆又党附于跛者，邀游妓馆 - After the two servants were dismissed, the cunning servant joined forces with the lame gatekeeper and frequented brothels; describes the servant's subsequent actions.
  • 跛者知其有伏机，阳使先往待，而阴告主人往捕 - The lame gatekeeper knew that the cunning servant had a hidden trap, so he pretended to send him ahead while secretly informing the master to go and catch him; describes the lame gatekeeper's strategy.
  • 故黠仆亦败 - Thus, the cunning servant also failed; summarizes the outcome.
  • 嗟乎！一州县官司阍耳，而此四人者，互相倾轧，至辗转多方而不已 - Alas! A mere gatekeeper for a county official, and yet these four people schemed against each other, going through various twists and turns without end; expresses disappointment in the behavior of the four individuals.
  • 黄雀螳螂之喻，兹其明验矣 - The metaphor of the cicada, the mantis, and the yellow bird is clearly demonstrated here; uses a well-known fable to illustrate the point.
  • 附记之以著世情之险 - I add this note to highlight the dangers of human nature; concludes the passage with a moral lesson.

  • 跛者 - Refers to a lame person, often used to describe someone who is physically disabled.
  • 针线人 - Refers to a needlewoman, a person skilled in sewing and embroidery.
  • 妓馆 - Refers to a brothel, a place where prostitutes worked.
  • 黄雀螳螂之喻 - Refers to the fable of the cicada, the mantis, and the yellow bird, which illustrates the concept of unintended consequences and the chain of events leading to a greater threat.
  • 世情之险 - Refers to the dangers and complexities of human nature and social interactions.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '今将托钵山东，天下岂有此无良人耶' is a rhetorical question that sets the tone for the narrative, expressing disbelief at the existence of such an unrighteous person.
  • This sequence '方相与太息，忽窗外呜呜有泣声，良久语曰' describes a moment of shared reflection followed by a sudden and emotional interruption.
  • The question '尔尚责人无良耶' challenges the narrator's initial assumption.
  • This long clause '尔家本有妇，见我在门前买花粉，诡言未娶，诳我父母，赘尔于家，尔无良否耶' details a series of actions and accusations, each ending with a rhetorical question.
  • This clause '我父母患疫，先后殁，别无亲属，尔据其宅，收其资，而棺衾祭葬俱草草，与死一奴婢同，尔无良否耶' describes the aftermath of the narrator's parents' death and the narrator's treatment of the deceased, again ending with a rhetorical question.
  • This clause '尔妇附粮艘寻至，入门与尔相诟厉，即欲逐我，既而知原是我家，尔衣食于我，乃暂容留，尔巧说百端，降我为妾，我苟求宁静，忍泪曲从，尔无良否耶' details the narrator's relationship with the narrator's wife and the subsequent reduction to the status of a concubine.
  • This clause '既据我宅，索我供给，又虐使我，呼我小名，动使伏地受杖，尔反代彼揿我项背，按我手足，叱我勿转侧，尔无良否耶' describes the ongoing mistreatment and abuse of the narrator.
  • This clause '越年余我财产衣饰剥削并尽，乃鬻我于西商，来相我时，我不肯出，又痛捶我，致我途穷自尽，尔无良否耶' describes the narrator's financial exploitation and eventual sale to a Western merchant, leading to the narrator's death.
  • This clause '我殁后不与一柳棺，不与一纸钱，复褫我敝衣，仅存一裤，裹以芦席，葬丛冢，尔无良否耶' describes the narrator's treatment after death, emphasizing the lack of proper burial rites.
  • This clause '吾诉于神明，今来取尔，尔尚责人无良耶' describes the narrator's appeal to the gods and the final accusation of unrighteousness.
  • This clause '其声哀厉，僮仆并闻，南士惊怖，瑟缩莫措一词' describes the emotional impact of the narrator's words on the listeners.
  • This clause '遽噭然仆地，余虑或牵涉，未晓即行，不知其后如何，谅无生理矣' describes the narrator's sudden collapse and the narrator's departure, with a sense of finality and resignation.

  • 神明 - Refers to the gods or divine beings.
  • 讲学家 - Refers to scholars or experts in Confucian learning.

  • 拥炉夜坐，冷不可支 - 'Huddled by the stove at night, the cold was unbearable'; '不可支' means 'unbearable' or 'too much to bear.'
  • 乃息烛就寝 - 'He then extinguished the lamp and went to bed'; a common sequence of actions in classical narrative.
  • 既觉，见承尘纸破处有光，异之 - 'After waking, he saw light coming from a hole in the ceiling paper, and found it strange'; '异之' means 'found it strange' or 'was surprised by it.'
  • 披衣潜起，就破处审视 - 'He put on his clothes and secretly rose to examine the hole'; '潜起' means 'to rise secretly' or 'to get up stealthily.'
  • 方艺火炊饭 - 'She was just kindling fire to cook rice'; '艺火' means 'to kindle fire' or 'to start a fire.'
  • 正凝视间，忽然一嚏 - 'As he was watching closely, suddenly she sneezed'; '正凝视间' is a temporal phrase meaning 'just as he was watching closely.'
  • 妇惊，触几灯覆，遂无所见 - 'The woman was startled, bumped the table, and overturned the lamp, so nothing could be seen'; this is a narrative sequence of events.
  • 晓起，破承尘视之 - 'In the morning, he broke open the ceiling to look at it'; '晓起' means 'to rise at dawn.'
  • 黄泥小灶，光洁异常 - 'A small stove made of yellow clay, unusually clean'; '光洁异常' means 'unusually clean' or 'exceptionally clean.'
  • 铁釜大如碗，饭犹未熟也 - 'An iron pot as large as a bowl, the rice still uncooked'; this is a contrast between the small size of the stove and the large pot.
  • 小锡檠倒置几下，油痕狼籍 - 'The small tin lampstand was overturned under the table, with oil stains everywhere'; '狼籍' means 'in disarray' or 'scattered in disorder.'
  • 惟蒸火处纸不燃，殊可怪耳 - 'Only the part where the steaming fire was did not burn, which was very strange'; '殊可怪耳' is a classical way of expressing 'very strange indeed.'
