[1370]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 戊寅五月二十八日 - Classical date format using the sexagenary cycle (戊寅 = 'Wu Yin') and lunar calendar date. '戊寅' may refer to the day or be part of a larger date.
  • 為壽 - Traditional expression meaning 'to offer a toast' or 'to celebrate a birthday.'
  • 煙戲 - Compound noun meaning 'smoke performance' or 'smoke magic.'
  • 口操南音 - 'Spoke with a southern accent' or 'used a southern dialect.'
  • 談吐風雅 - 'Spoke and behaved in a refined and elegant manner.'
  • 不知其何以戲也 - 'One did not know how he performed the trick.'
  • 俄有仆携巨烟筒来 - 'Soon, a servant brought a large pipe.'
  • 中可受烟四两 - 'Could hold four liang (a traditional Chinese unit of weight, roughly 50 grams) of tobacco.'
  • 燃火吸之，且吸且咽 - 'Lit it and inhaled, inhaling and swallowing at the same time.'
  • 食顷方尽 - 'After a meal's time, it was finished.' '食顷' is a classical expression meaning 'the time it takes to eat a meal,' i.e., about an hour.'
  • 索巨碗沦苦茗饮讫 - 'He then asked for a large bowl of bitter tea and drank it.' '沦' is a variant of '沦' (to brew), and '苦茗' is 'bitter tea,' often associated with meditation or austerity.'
  • 為君添鹤算，可乎？ - 'Shall I add to your years of the crane?' '添鹤算' is a classical idiom referring to adding years of life, with the crane (鹤) being a symbol of longevity.'
  • 即张吻吐鹤二只 - 'He then opened his mouth and spat out two cranes.' '张吻' is a literal description of opening the mouth.'
  • 飞向屋角，徐吐一圈 - 'They flew toward the corner of the room, and he slowly exhaled a circle.' '徐' means 'slowly.'
  • 大如盘，双鹤穿之而过 - 'As large as a plate, with the two cranes passing through it.' '盘' is a plate or dish.'
  • 往来飞舞，如掷梭然 - 'Flying back and forth, dancing like a shuttle.' '掷梭' is a metaphor for the motion of a weaver’s shuttle.'
  • 既而嘎喉有声，吐烟如一线 - 'Afterward, he made a sound in his throat and exhaled a thin line of smoke.' '嘎喉' may be a phonetic description of a sound made in the throat.'
  • 亭亭直上，散作水波云状 - 'Straight up into the air, dispersing into the shape of water ripples and clouds.' '亭亭' means 'upright' or 'standing tall.'
  • 谛视皆寸许小鹤 - 'Upon close inspection, they were small cranes about an inch in size.' '寸许' is a classical way of saying 'about an inch.'
  • 翩翩左右，移时方灭 - 'Dancing left and right, they disappeared after some time.' '移时' means 'after a while.'
  • 众皆以为目所未睹也 - 'All the guests believed they had never seen anything like this.' '目所未睹' is a classical expression meaning 'never seen with one's own eyes.'
  • 俄其弟子继至奉一觞与主人曰 - 'Soon after, his disciple arrived and presented a cup to the host, saying...' '觞' is a wine cup.'
  • 吾技不如师，为君小作剧可乎？ - 'My skill is not as good as my master’s; may I perform a small trick for you?' '小作剧' is a modest way of saying 'perform a small magic trick.'
  • 呼吸间，有朵云缥缈筵前 - 'In a breath, a cloud appeared, drifting before the banquet.' '缥缈' is 'ethereal' or 'faint and indistinct.'
  • 徐结成小楼阁，雕栏绮窗，历历如画 - 'Gradually forming a small pavilion, with carved railings and ornate windows, clearly like a painting.' '历历如画' means 'clearly like a painting.'
  • 曰：此海屋添筹也 - 'He said: This is the 'Sea House Adding Sticks' [a metaphor for adding years to life].' '海屋添筹' is a classical idiom referring to adding years to life, often associated with immortality or longevity.'
  • 诸客复大惊 - 'The guests were once again greatly surprised.' '复' means 'again.'
  • 以为指上毫光，现玲珑塔，亦无以喻是矣 - 'They thought it was the light on the tip of a finger manifesting a crystal tower, but no words could describe it.' '玲珑塔' is a 'crystal tower' or 'jeweled pagoda,' a metaphor for something intricate and beautiful.'
  • 以余所见诸说部，如掷杯化鹤，顷刻开花之类，不可殚述 - 'Based on what I have seen in various stories, such as turning cups into cranes or flowers blooming in an instant, there are too many to recount.' '说部' refers to narrative literature or stories.'
  • 毋亦实有其事？ - 'Is it not that such things actually exist?' A rhetorical question suggesting disbelief in the face of the extraordinary.
  • 后之人少所见，多所怪乎？ - 'Later people see little of it and are therefore surprised?' '少所见' means 'see little of it,' and '多所怪' means 'are often surprised.'
  • 如此事非余目睹，亦终不信也 - 'If I had not seen this with my own eyes, I would never have believed it.' A final statement affirming the truth of the experience.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 戊寅 - A term from the traditional Chinese sexagenary cycle. It refers to the combination of the heavenly stem 'Wu' (戊) and the earthly branch 'Yin' (寅), used to mark years, months, or days.
  • 烟戏 - A form of traditional Chinese performance or magic involving smoke, possibly related to fire breathing or breath control.
  • 南音 - A southern dialect or style of speech, possibly referring to the dialect of the southern provinces or the style of southern music.
  • 鹤 - The crane, a symbol of longevity, immortality, and purity in Chinese culture.
  • 添鹤算 - A metaphor for adding years to life, often associated with longevity and immortality.
  • 海屋添筹 - A classical idiom referring to the 'Sea House Adding Sticks' (海屋添筹), a metaphor for adding years to life, often depicted as a house by the sea with sticks (or tally sticks) being added to a counter.
  • 说部 - A term for narrative literature or stories, often in the form of novels or folktales.
  • 掷杯化鹤 - A reference to a legend or story where a cup is transformed into a crane, a motif found in Chinese folklore.
  • 顷刻开花 - A phrase meaning 'flowers bloom in an instant,' often used to describe miraculous or magical transformations.
